Aortic Dissection.who_gets_it: Older men with HTN; younger patients with Marfan/Ehlers-Danlos.
Aortic Dissection.risk_factors: Chronic HTN, Bicuspid aortic valve, Cocaine use.
Aortic Dissection.typical_presentation: Sudden onset 'tearing' chest pain radiating to the back.
Aortic Dissection.moa: Intimal tear creates a false lumen in the aortic wall.
Aortic Dissection.step1_buzzwords: Tearing/Ripping pain, BP discrepancy between arms, Mediastinal widening.
Aortic Dissection.lab_buzzwords: Markedly elevated D-Dimer (rules out if negative).

Appendicitis.typical_presentation: Periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ, fever, anorexia.
Appendicitis.moa: Obstruction of appendiceal lumen leads to bacterial overgrowth.
Appendicitis.step1_buzzwords: McBurney's point, Rovsing sign, Psoas sign, Obturator sign.

Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma.risk_factors: Mydriatics (pupil dilation), dark rooms, Sulfa drugs.
Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma.typical_presentation: Sudden eye pain, blurry vision, 'halos' around lights, nausea.
Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma.moa: Iris blocks trabecular meshwork; rapid intraocular pressure rise.
Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma.step1_buzzwords: Steamy cornea, Mid-dilated fixed pupil, Rock-hard eye.

Guillain-Barre Syndrome (GBS).who_gets_it: Any age, usually 1-2 weeks after an infection.
Guillain-Barre Syndrome (GBS).typical_presentation: Symmetric ascending muscle weakness and loss of reflexes.
Guillain-Barre Syndrome (GBS).moa: Autoimmune demyelination of peripheral nerves via molecular mimicry.
Guillain-Barre Syndrome (GBS).lab_buzzwords: Albuminocytologic dissociation (High protein/Normal WBC in CSF).

Acute Cholecystitis.who_gets_it: The 4 F's: Female, Fat, Fertile, Forty.
Acute Cholecystitis.typical_presentation: Constant RUQ pain, right shoulder radiation, fever, vomiting.
Acute Cholecystitis.moa: Gallstone obstruction of the cystic duct causing inflammation.

Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM).risk_factors: Autosomal Dominant; beta-myosin heavy chain mutations.
Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM).typical_presentation: Dyspnea on exertion, syncope, sudden death in athletes.
Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM).moa: Asymmetric septal hypertrophy causing LVOT obstruction.
Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM).step1_buzzwords: Systolic Anterior Motion (SAM), S4, Murmur softer with squatting.

Pulmonary Hypertension.risk_factors: BMPR2 mutation, Connective tissue disease, Left heart failure.
Pulmonary Hypertension.typical_presentation: Progressive exertional dyspnea, Raynaud's, precordial heave.
Pulmonary Hypertension.moa: Proliferation of pulmonary artery smooth muscle; plexiform lesions.
Pulmonary Hypertension.step1_buzzwords: Loud S2, Plexiform lesions, Right ventricular heave.

Hyperparathyroidism (Primary).who_gets_it: Postmenopausal women; MEN 1 and MEN 2A families.
Hyperparathyroidism (Primary).moa: Overproduction of PTH leading to excessive bone resorption.
Hyperparathyroidism (Primary).step1_buzzwords: Osteitis fibrosa cystica, Salt and pepper skull, Subperiosteal resorption.
Hyperparathyroidism (Primary).lab_buzzwords: High PTH, High Calcium, Low Phosphorus.

Squamous Cell Lung Carcinoma.typical_presentation: Cough, hemoptysis, hilar mass, right arm edema (SVC syndrome).
Squamous Cell Lung Carcinoma.moa: Malignant transformation of bronchial squamous metaplasia.
Squamous Cell Lung Carcinoma.step1_buzzwords: Hilar mass, Keratin pearls, Intercellular bridges.

Acute Intermittent Porphyria.risk_factors: Alcohol, Sulfa drugs, starvation, Cytochrome P450 inducers.
Acute Intermittent Porphyria.typical_presentation: Abdominal pain (no tenderness), confusion, dark urine.
Acute Intermittent Porphyria.step1_buzzwords: 5 P's: Painful abdomen, Port-wine urine, Polyneuropathy, Psychological distress, Precipitated by drugs.

Prostate Cancer.risk_factors: Age, African American race, High-fat diet.
Prostate Cancer.typical_presentation: Often asymptomatic early. Metastatic: back pain, neurologic deficits (spinal cord compression), urinary incontinence, blastic bone lesions.
Prostate Cancer.moa: Adenocarcinoma of the posterior lobe (peripheral zone).
Prostate Cancer.step1_buzzwords: Peripheral zone, Blastic (sclerotic) bone mets, Elevated ALP, PSA, Gleason score.

Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever.who_gets_it: Hikers/Campers in NC, TN, OK, AR.
Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever.typical_presentation: High fever, headache, rash on wrists/palms moving inward.
Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever.moa: Rickettsia rickettsii obligate intracellular infection of endothelial cells.
Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever.step1_buzzwords: Centripetal rash (outer to inner), Palms and Soles rash.

Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML).risk_factors: Prior radiation, Alkylating chemo, Down Syndrome.
Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML).typical_presentation: Fatigue, easy bruising, bleeding gums, bone pain.
Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML).step1_buzzwords: Auer rods, MPO positive, Gingival hyperplasia (M5).

Hepatitis A.typical_presentation: Jaundice, RUQ pain, fever, nausea after travel.
Hepatitis A.moa: Picornavirus causing acute hepatocyte swelling and necrosis.

Hypothyroidism.typical_presentation: Cold intolerance, weight gain, fatigue, dry skin, constipation.
Hypothyroidism.step1_buzzwords: Delayed reflex relaxation, Hurthle cells (Hashimoto's), Puffy face.
Hypothyroidism.lab_buzzwords: High TSH, Low T4, Anti-TPO antibodies.

Pheochromocytoma.typical_presentation: Episodic 5 P's: Pressure (HTN), Pain (Headache), Perspiration, Palpitations, Pallor.
Pheochromocytoma.moa: Tumor of chromaffin cells in adrenal medulla.
Pheochromocytoma.step1_buzzwords: Chromaffin cells, VMA, Phenoxybenzamine (alpha-block before beta).
Pheochromocytoma.treatment: Alpha-blocker followed by Beta-blocker, then surgical resection.

Esophageal Squamous Cell Carcinoma.risk_factors: Alcohol, Smoking, Achalasia, Caustic injury, Hot liquids.
Esophageal Squamous Cell Carcinoma.typical_presentation: Progressive dysphagia (solids then liquids), weight loss.
Esophageal Squamous Cell Carcinoma.moa: Malignant growth in upper/middle 1/3 of esophagus.
Esophageal Squamous Cell Carcinoma.lab_buzzwords: Biopsy: Squamous cells with keratin pearls.

Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH).who_gets_it: Adults, often associated with Berry aneurysms.
Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH).moa: Rupture of an aneurysm in the subarachnoid space.
Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH).step1_buzzwords: Worst headache of life, Berry aneurysm, Xanthochromia.

Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE).who_gets_it: Females of reproductive age; African American/Hispanic.
Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE).typical_presentation: Malar rash, joint pain, photosensitivity, renal issues.
Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE).moa: Type III hypersensitivity; autoantibodies against nuclear antigens.
Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE).step1_buzzwords: Malar/Butterfly rash, Wire-loop lesions (kidney), Libman-Sacks endocarditis.

Stable Angina.who_gets_it: Older adults with CAD risk factors.
Stable Angina.typical_presentation: Chest pain on exertion relieved by rest or nitroglycerin.
Stable Angina.moa: Atherosclerosis causing >70% narrowing of coronary arteries.
Stable Angina.step1_buzzwords: Exertional pain, ST depression on EKG.

Prerenal Acute Kidney Injury (NSAID-Indu.typical_presentation: Decreased urine output, confusion, dry mucous membranes.
Prerenal Acute Kidney Injury (NSAID-Indu.moa: NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandins, causing afferent arteriole constriction.

Uncomplicated Cystitis (E. coli).moa: Ascending infection of the bladder by fecal flora.

Multiple Myeloma.typical_presentation: Bone pain, fractures, infections, fatigue, renal failure.
Multiple Myeloma.moa: Monoclonal plasma cell proliferation in the bone marrow.
Multiple Myeloma.step1_buzzwords: CRAB: Calcium high, Renal failure, Anemia, Bone lesions.

Myasthenia Gravis.typical_presentation: Ptosis, diplopia, muscle weakness that worsens with use.

Ischemic Stroke.who_gets_it: Elderly with HTN, AFib, or CAD.
Ischemic Stroke.moa: Embolic or thrombotic occlusion of a cerebral artery.
Ischemic Stroke.lab_buzzwords: Normal CT head (initially), DWI MRI positive.

Alzheimer Disease.typical_presentation: Slowly progressive memory loss, disorientation, paranoia.
Alzheimer Disease.moa: Beta-amyloid plaques and Tau neurofibrillary tangles.
Alzheimer Disease.step1_buzzwords: Amyloid plaques, Neurofibrillary tangles, Hippocampal atrophy.

Bacterial Meningitis.typical_presentation: Fever, headache, stiff neck, altered mental status.
Bacterial Meningitis.moa: Infection of the leptomeninges by bacteria.
Bacterial Meningitis.lab_buzzwords: CSF: Low Glucose, High Protein, High PMNs (neutrophils).

Congestive Heart Failure (CHF).risk_factors: HTN, Ischemic heart disease, Valvular disease.
Congestive Heart Failure (CHF).typical_presentation: Dyspnea, orthopnea, PND, bilateral leg edema.
Congestive Heart Failure (CHF).moa: Heart fails to pump blood efficiently; backward failure.
Congestive Heart Failure (CHF).step1_buzzwords: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Dyspnea, S3, Kerley B lines.

Urolithiasis (Kidney Stones).typical_presentation: Sudden flank pain radiating to groin (colicky).
Urolithiasis (Kidney Stones).moa: Supersaturation of urine leading to crystal formation.

Carcinoid Syndrome.typical_presentation: Diarrhea, Flushing, Wheezing, Right-heart valve lesions.
Carcinoid Syndrome.moa: Tumor secretes Serotonin after metastasizing to liver.

Testicular Torsion.typical_presentation: Acute severe testicular pain, high-riding testis, nausea.
Testicular Torsion.moa: Twisting of the spermatic cord cutting off blood supply.

Celiac Disease.who_gets_it: All ages; associated with Down Syndrome, Type 1 DM.
Celiac Disease.typical_presentation: Diarrhea, bloating, dermatitis herpetiformis, iron deficiency.
Celiac Disease.moa: Immune-mediated damage to small bowel villi due to gluten.

Pseudomonas Corneal Ulcer (Contact Lens-.typical_presentation: Eye pain, redness, blurry vision, hypopyon.
Pseudomonas Corneal Ulcer (Contact Lens-.moa: Bacterial infection of the corneal stroma.

Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML).typical_presentation: Fatigue, weight loss, massive splenomegaly, early satiety.
Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML).moa: t(9;22) BCR-ABL fusion creates a constitutive tyrosine kinase.
Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML).lab_buzzwords: Low LAP score (differentiates from leukemoid reaction).

Peripartum Cardiomyopathy.risk_factors: Last month of pregnancy or first 5 months postpartum.
Peripartum Cardiomyopathy.typical_presentation: Dyspnea, edema, orthopnea shortly after delivery.
Peripartum Cardiomyopathy.moa: Dilated cardiomyopathy of unknown etiology in late pregnancy.
Peripartum Cardiomyopathy.treatment: Heart failure meds (standard), avoid future pregnancy.

Urosepsis (Obstructive Pyelonephritis).who_gets_it: Elderly, patients with stones or BPH.
Urosepsis (Obstructive Pyelonephritis).moa: Infection above an obstruction entering the bloodstream.
Urosepsis (Obstructive Pyelonephritis).lab_buzzwords: WBC casts in urine (localizes to kidney).

Acute Pancreatitis.typical_presentation: Epigastric pain radiating to back, nausea, vomiting.
Acute Pancreatitis.moa: Autodigestion of pancreas by premature activation of trypsin.
Acute Pancreatitis.step1_buzzwords: Radiates to back, Cullen sign, Grey Turner sign.
Acute Pancreatitis.treatment: NPO, Aggressive IV fluids, Pain control.

Sarcoidosis.typical_presentation: Dry cough, dyspnea, erythema nodosum, uveitis.
Sarcoidosis.step1_buzzwords: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, Erythema nodosum, ACE level.
Sarcoidosis.lab_buzzwords: High Calcium, High ACE, High Vitamin D.

Peritonsillar Abscess.typical_presentation: Severe sore throat, muffled 'hot potato' voice, trismus.
Peritonsillar Abscess.moa: Collection of pus between tonsillar capsule and pharyngeal constrictors.
Peritonsillar Abscess.step1_buzzwords: Hot potato voice, Uvular deviation, Trismus.

Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA).moa: Pannus formation and destruction of cartilage/bone.

Osteomyelitis.who_gets_it: IV drug users, diabetics, sickle cell patients.
Osteomyelitis.moa: Hematogenous or contiguous spread of infection to bone.
Osteomyelitis.step1_buzzwords: IV drug user, S. aureus, Sequestrum.

Anorexia Nervosa.typical_presentation: Low BMI, amenorrhea, lanugo, fear of weight gain.
Anorexia Nervosa.moa: Severe caloric restriction and distorted body image.

Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus.typical_presentation: Wet, Wacky, and Wobbly (Incontinence, Dementia, Ataxia).
Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus.moa: Impaired CSF absorption; ventriculomegaly without high pressure.
Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus.lab_buzzwords: Gait improvement after Large Volume Tap.

Dermatomyositis.typical_presentation: Proximal muscle weakness, eyelid rash, knuckle rash.
Dermatomyositis.step1_buzzwords: Heliotrope rash, Gottron papules, Shawl sign.

Myocardial Infarction (Heart Attack).typical_presentation: Crushing substernal chest pain, jaw/arm radiation, diaphoresis.
Myocardial Infarction (Heart Attack).moa: Coronary artery occlusion causing myocyte necrosis.
Myocardial Infarction (Heart Attack).step1_buzzwords: ST elevation, Coagulative necrosis, Jaw radiation.

Ulcerative Colitis (UC).moa: Continuous mucosal inflammation starting in the rectum.
Ulcerative Colitis (UC).step1_buzzwords: Lead pipe colon, Crypt abscesses, Rectal involvement.

Disseminated Gonococcal Infection (Septi.moa: Systemic spread of N. gonorrhoeae from mucosal site.

Parkinson Disease.typical_presentation: Resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, postural instability.
Parkinson Disease.moa: Loss of dopaminergic neurons in the Substantia Nigra.

Gilbert Syndrome.lab_buzzwords: Normal LFTs, Isolated high indirect bilirubin.

Alcoholic Cardiomyopathy.typical_presentation: Heart failure (SOB, edema) in an alcoholic.
Alcoholic Cardiomyopathy.moa: Direct toxic effect of ethanol on myocardium (Dilated CM).

Retinal Detachment.typical_presentation: Gray curtain over vision, flashes (photopsia), floaters.
Retinal Detachment.moa: Separation of neurosensory retina from pigment epithelium.

Pulmonary Embolism (PE).risk_factors: Virchow Triad: Stasis, Hypercoagulability, Endothelial injury.
Pulmonary Embolism (PE).typical_presentation: Sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia.
Pulmonary Embolism (PE).moa: Thrombus (usually from DVT) lodges in pulmonary artery.

Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis.risk_factors: Recent Group A Strep infection (Pharyngitis or Impetigo).
Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis.moa: Type III hypersensitivity; immune complex deposition.

Zenker Diverticulum.typical_presentation: Halitosis, regurgitation of undigested food, dysphagia.
Zenker Diverticulum.step1_buzzwords: Halitosis, Hot potato voice (if huge), Killian triangle.

C. Difficile Infection.typical_presentation: Profuse watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever.
C. Difficile Infection.moa: Toxin A (brush border) and Toxin B (cytoskeleton) damage.

Primary Biliary Cholangitis.moa: Autoimmune destruction of intrahepatic bile ducts.

Cauda Equina Syndrome.who_gets_it: Adults with disc disease or tumors.
Cauda Equina Syndrome.typical_presentation: Saddle anesthesia, bowel/bladder dysfunction, leg weakness.
Cauda Equina Syndrome.moa: Compression of multiple lumbosacral nerve roots.

Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (Chlamydia-A.risk_factors: Multiple partners, lack of barrier protection.
Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (Chlamydia-A.typical_presentation: Cervical motion tenderness, adnexal pain, discharge.
Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (Chlamydia-A.moa: Ascending infection of the upper genital tract.

Aortic Stenosis.moa: Narrowing of the aortic valve orifice.

Adult-Onset Still Disease.typical_presentation: Daily spiking fevers, evanescent salmon rash, arthritis.

Gout.moa: Precipitation of monosodium urate crystals in joints.

Anti-MDA5 Dermatomyositis.who_gets_it: Adults; specifically seen in East Asian populations.
Anti-MDA5 Dermatomyositis.typical_presentation: Knuckle rash, skin ulcers, rapidly progressive lung disease.
Anti-MDA5 Dermatomyositis.moa: Autoimmune attack on skin and lung vasculature.

Aortic Dissection (Marfan Syndrome).typical_presentation: Tearing chest pain, lens dislocation, hypermobility.
Aortic Dissection (Marfan Syndrome).moa: Cystic medial necrosis of the aorta.

Malignant Pleural Mesothelioma.typical_presentation: Dyspnea, chest pain, weight loss, pleural effusions.

Anti-NMDA Receptor Encephalitis.moa: Autoantibodies against the GluN1 subunit of NMDA receptor.

Disseminated Histoplasmosis.who_gets_it: Residents of Ohio/Mississippi river valleys; HIV patients.
Disseminated Histoplasmosis.step1_buzzwords: Histo hides in macrophages, Ohio River Valley.

Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS).typical_presentation: Abdominal pain relieved by defecation, bloating.
Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS).moa: Altered gut motility and visceral hypersensitivity.

Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS).typical_presentation: Mixed UMN and LMN signs, fasciculations, normal sensation.
Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS).moa: Degeneration of upper and lower motor neurons.

Small Cell Lung Cancer.typical_presentation: Cough, hemoptysis, weight loss, SVC syndrome (facial swelling, arm edema), Horner syndrome.
Small Cell Lung Cancer.moa: Neuroendocrine tumor of Kulchitsky cells; early hematogenous spread.
Small Cell Lung Cancer.step1_buzzwords: Oat cells, Nuclear molding, SVC syndrome, SIADH, Lambert-Eaton, ACTH (Cushing).
Small Cell Lung Cancer.lab_buzzwords: Hyponatremia (SIADH), Synaptophysin/Chromogranin A positive on biopsy.
Small Cell Lung Cancer.treatment: Chemotherapy (Cisplatin + Etoposide) + Radiation. Surgery rarely indicated — too advanced at diagnosis.

Aortic Regurgitation.who_gets_it: Adults with bicuspid aortic valve, Marfan syndrome, or history of endocarditis or rheumatic fever.
Aortic Regurgitation.risk_factors: Bicuspid aortic valve (most common in young), Marfan syndrome, aortic root dilation, prior endocarditis.
Aortic Regurgitation.typical_presentation: Slowly progressive exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, wide pulse pressure, bounding pulses — often asymptomatic for years before decompensation.
Aortic Regurgitation.moa: Regurgitant flow from aorta back into LV during diastole causes LV volume overload → eccentric hypertrophy → eventual systolic dysfunction.
Aortic Regurgitation.step1_buzzwords: Water-hammer pulse, de Musset's sign, wide pulse pressure, diastolic blowing murmur, Austin Flint murmur.
Aortic Regurgitation.lab_buzzwords: Echo: regurgitant fraction, vena contracta, LVEDP elevated on cath.
Aortic Regurgitation.treatment: Surgical AVR (valve replacement) — indicated when symptoms develop or EF drops below 50-55% or LV dilates >65mm.

Hereditary Hemochromatosis.who_gets_it: Adults of Northern European descent, typically males presenting in 40s-50s (females present later due to menstrual iron loss).
Hereditary Hemochromatosis.risk_factors: Homozygous HFE C282Y mutation, family history, high dietary iron intake.
Hereditary Hemochromatosis.typical_presentation: Bronze skin, cirrhosis, diabetes mellitus, arthropathy (2nd/3rd MCP), hypogonadism, cardiomyopathy — the classic pentad.
Hereditary Hemochromatosis.moa: HFE mutation → unregulated intestinal iron absorption → iron deposits in liver, heart, pancreas, joints, and pituitary.
Hereditary Hemochromatosis.step1_buzzwords: Bronze diabetes, C282Y mutation, Prussian blue stain, transferrin saturation >45%, elevated ferritin.
Hereditary Hemochromatosis.lab_buzzwords: Ferritin >1000 ng/mL, transferrin saturation >45%, elevated LFTs, elevated glucose.
Hereditary Hemochromatosis.treatment: Therapeutic phlebotomy (weekly until ferritin <50) — removes excess iron. Chelation if phlebotomy not tolerated.

Addison's Disease.mechanism: Autoimmune destruction of the adrenal cortex (>90% of tissue) causes deficiency of all three adrenal hormones: cortisol (glucocorticoid), aldosterone (mineralocorticoid), and DHEA (androgen). Without aldosterone, sodium is wasted and potassium is retained — causing the classic hyponatremia + hyperkalemia electrolyte pattern. Without cortisol, the pituitary's negative feedback is lost, causing ACTH to rise dramatically. ACTH has structural homology with MSH (melanocyte-stimulating hormone) — explaining the pathognomonic hyperpigmentation, especially in sun-exposed areas, palmar creases, buccal mucosa, and scars.
Addison's Disease.diagnosis: Diagnosis requires a cosyntropin (ACTH) stimulation test: administer 250mcg synthetic ACTH and measure cortisol at 0, 30, and 60 minutes. Peak cortisol <18 µg/dL = adrenal insufficiency. A markedly elevated ACTH level (>200 pg/mL) with low cortisol confirms primary (adrenal) disease — distinguishing it from secondary (pituitary) insufficiency where ACTH would be low. 21-hydroxylase antibodies confirm autoimmune etiology (Addison's disease). The 'triple threat' triad: hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + hypoglycemia in a sick, hyperpigmented patient = Addison's until proven otherwise.
Addison's Disease.treatment: Acute adrenal crisis: IV hydrocortisone 100mg bolus → 50-100mg q6-8h + aggressive IV saline (aldosterone deficiency causes volume depletion). Chronic management: hydrocortisone (mimics diurnal cortisol rhythm — highest dose in morning) + fludrocortisone (synthetic mineralocorticoid to replace aldosterone). Patients must double or triple their dose during illness, surgery, or physical stress ('sick day rules'). Medical alert bracelet and emergency IM hydrocortisone kit are mandatory — adrenal crisis is rapidly fatal without treatment.

Amyloidosis (AL).mechanism: In AL (light chain) amyloidosis, clonal plasma cells in the bone marrow overproduce immunoglobulin light chains (most commonly lambda). These light chains misfold and aggregate into beta-pleated sheet fibrils — amyloid — which deposit in organs and disrupt their architecture. The heart is the most critical organ: amyloid infiltrates the myocardium, causing severe diastolic dysfunction and a 'restrictive cardiomyopathy' pattern. Paradoxically, the massively thickened walls on echo (LVH appearance) are accompanied by low voltage on EKG — amyloid fibrils are electrically insulating. This low voltage + 'thick walls' discordance is virtually pathognomonic. Factor X deficiency (amyloid binds and sequesters factor X) and periorbital ecchymosis ('raccoon eyes' from minor trauma or Valsalva) are also characteristic. Macroglossia occurs when amyloid deposits in the tongue.
Amyloidosis (AL).diagnosis: Serum free light chain ratio (kappa/lambda) is the most sensitive screening test — a markedly abnormal ratio confirms the clonal process. UPEP shows Bence Jones proteinuria (free light chains). Fat pad biopsy (abdominal subcutaneous fat): Congo Red staining shows apple-green birefringence under polarized light — pathognomonic for all amyloid types. Bone marrow biopsy quantifies plasma cell burden. EKG low voltage + echo 'sparkling/granular' myocardium + restrictive filling = cardiac amyloid until proven otherwise. Technetium pyrophosphate (Tc-PYP) scan is positive in ATTR amyloid but negative in AL — helps distinguish the two.
Amyloidosis (AL).treatment: AL amyloidosis: Treat the underlying plasma cell dyscrasia with daratumumab-based regimens (similar to myeloma). Cardiac transplant followed by stem cell transplant in selected patients. ATTR amyloidosis (transthyretin — separate disease): tafamidis stabilizes TTF tetramer and slows progression. Unlike myeloma, AL amyloid is not cured by chemotherapy — organ damage from amyloid deposits is largely irreversible. Prognosis is driven by cardiac involvement: median survival without treatment ~6 months with advanced cardiac disease; modern therapy extends this significantly.

Boerhaave Syndrome.who_gets_it: Middle-aged alcoholic males; any patient with forceful/prolonged vomiting.
Boerhaave Syndrome.risk_factors: Alcohol intoxication, eating disorders (bulimia), forceful vomiting.
Boerhaave Syndrome.typical_presentation: Sudden severe chest and back pain immediately after violent vomiting; diaphoresis, subcutaneous emphysema at neck.
Boerhaave Syndrome.moa: Sudden spike in intraesophageal pressure causes full-thickness longitudinal tear at the left posterolateral GEJ — gastric contents flood the mediastinum causing chemical then bacterial mediastinitis.
Boerhaave Syndrome.step1_buzzwords: Mackler triad (vomiting + chest pain + subcutaneous emphysema), Hamman's sign (mediastinal crunch), pneumomediastinum.
Boerhaave Syndrome.lab_buzzwords: Pleural fluid: very low pH (<6), elevated amylase (salivary isoenzyme — NOT lipase). CT: oral contrast extravasation.
Boerhaave Syndrome.treatment: Emergency surgical repair within 24 hours. Broad-spectrum antibiotics + chest drainage. Esophageal stenting for poor surgical candidates. Mortality >50% if delayed.

MELAS Syndrome.who_gets_it: Children and young adults; maternal inheritance pattern (mitochondrial DNA).
MELAS Syndrome.risk_factors: m.3243A>G mutation in MT-TL1 gene (tRNA-Leu) — most common. Variable heteroplasmy determines severity.
MELAS Syndrome.typical_presentation: Stroke-like episodes before age 40 NOT following vascular territories, lactic acidosis, encephalopathy, sensorineural hearing loss, short stature, diabetes, WPW pattern.
MELAS Syndrome.moa: Mitochondrial DNA mutation impairs oxidative phosphorylation — neurons with high energy demand fail, causing stroke-like episodes from metabolic failure rather than ischemia.
MELAS Syndrome.step1_buzzwords: Ragged red fibers (Gomori trichrome), maternal inheritance, stroke not following vascular territory, elevated lactate:pyruvate ratio >20.
MELAS Syndrome.lab_buzzwords: Lactate elevated at rest, MR spectroscopy: lactate peak at 1.33 ppm, m.3243A>G on genetic testing, muscle biopsy ragged red fibers.
MELAS Syndrome.treatment: No cure. L-arginine IV during stroke-like episodes (increases NO, improves vasodilation). CoQ10, riboflavin supplements. Avoid valproate (worsens mitochondrial function).

Menetrier Disease.who_gets_it: Adults 40-60, males >> females. CMV-associated in children.
Menetrier Disease.risk_factors: TGF-alpha overexpression, possible H. pylori association (though negative in most).
Menetrier Disease.typical_presentation: Epigastric pain, profound weight loss, steatorrhea, bilateral pitting edema from hypoalbuminemia. PPI-refractory. H. pylori negative.
Menetrier Disease.moa: TGF-alpha overexpression causes massive foveolar hyperplasia and parietal cell loss — achlorhydria + massive protein leakage into gastric lumen = protein-losing gastropathy.
Menetrier Disease.step1_buzzwords: Giant gastric rugal folds (fundus/body, antrum SPARED), protein-losing gastropathy, achlorhydria, antrum sparing.
Menetrier Disease.lab_buzzwords: Albumin critically low (<2.0), elevated fecal alpha-1 antitrypsin clearance (confirms protein-losing gastropathy), full-thickness biopsy shows foveolar hyperplasia with gland atrophy.
Menetrier Disease.treatment: Cetuximab (EGFR/TGF-alpha inhibitor). Gastrectomy for refractory cases. High-protein diet. Treat H. pylori if positive.

Lemierre Syndrome.who_gets_it: Healthy young adults (15-35) — the 'forgotten disease.' Often misdiagnosed as EBV or strep.
Lemierre Syndrome.risk_factors: Fusobacterium necrophorum oropharyngeal colonization, prior pharyngitis, poor dental hygiene.
Lemierre Syndrome.typical_presentation: Pharyngitis → tender neck mass (IJV thrombosis) → septic pulmonary emboli (cavitating nodules). Strep and mono tests negative. Young healthy patient in septic shock.
Lemierre Syndrome.moa: Fusobacterium necrophorum spreads from oropharynx to parapharyngeal space → internal jugular vein thrombophlebitis → septic emboli to lungs, liver, joints.
Lemierre Syndrome.step1_buzzwords: Fusobacterium necrophorum, IJV thrombosis, septic pulmonary emboli, young adult with pharyngitis + neck mass.
Lemierre Syndrome.lab_buzzwords: Blood cultures: gram-negative anaerobic rods. CT neck: IJV thrombosis + parapharyngeal abscess. CT chest: bilateral cavitating nodules.
Lemierre Syndrome.treatment: Prolonged antibiotics x4-6 weeks: metronidazole + beta-lactam/BLI (ampicillin-sulbactam). Anticoagulation controversial — used in extensive thrombosis. I&D of abscess if present.

Stiff Person Syndrome.who_gets_it: Middle-aged females. Associated with type 1 diabetes, autoimmune thyroiditis, vitiligo (autoimmune polyglandular).
Stiff Person Syndrome.risk_factors: Anti-GAD65 autoantibodies (loss of GABAergic inhibitory interneurons). Paraneoplastic (anti-amphiphysin) in breast/lung cancer.
Stiff Person Syndrome.typical_presentation: Progressive axial rigidity (board-like abdomen even at rest), painful episodic spasms triggered by noise/touch/emotion, falls without warning, hyperreflexia. Rigidity persists during sleep.
Stiff Person Syndrome.moa: Anti-GAD65 antibodies inhibit GABA synthesis — loss of inhibitory tone causes continuous motor unit firing and exaggerated startle.
Stiff Person Syndrome.step1_buzzwords: Anti-GAD65, continuous motor unit activity on EMG (does not suppress with sleep), exaggerated startle, dramatic benzodiazepine response.
Stiff Person Syndrome.lab_buzzwords: Anti-GAD65 >1000 IU/mL (vs. low titers in T1DM alone). EMG: continuous motor unit activity at rest. CSF: oligoclonal bands.
Stiff Person Syndrome.treatment: Benzodiazepines (dramatic response — diagnostic). Baclofen. IVIG or plasmapheresis for refractory cases. Treat underlying malignancy if paraneoplastic.

Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiom.who_gets_it: Young athletes; males > females. Leading cause of SCD in athletes in Italy.
Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiom.risk_factors: Autosomal dominant desmosomal mutations — PKP2 (plakophilin-2) most common. Exercise accelerates disease progression.
Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiom.typical_presentation: Syncope or SCD during exercise in a young athlete. LBBB-morphology PVCs. Family history of sudden death. RV dilation on echo.
Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiom.moa: Desmosomal protein mutations cause fibrofatty replacement of the RV myocardium — reentrant arrhythmias originate from the 'triangle of dysplasia' (inlet, apex, infundibulum).
Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiom.step1_buzzwords: Epsilon wave (V1-V3), T-wave inversions V1-V4, LBBB-morphology VT, fatty RV infiltration on cardiac MRI, triangle of dysplasia.
Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiom.lab_buzzwords: EKG: epsilon wave + T inversions V1-V4. Cardiac MRI: fatty infiltration + late gadolinium enhancement of RV free wall. PKP2 mutation on genetics.
Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiom.treatment: ICD implantation (all symptomatic patients). Antiarrhythmics (sotalol, amiodarone). Exercise prohibition — critical. Catheter ablation. Family screening mandatory.

Hereditary Angioedema.who_gets_it: Any age — autosomal dominant. Family history of angioedema. Episodes begin in childhood/adolescence.
Hereditary Angioedema.risk_factors: SERPING1 gene mutation. ACE inhibitors precipitate attacks (absolutely contraindicated). Estrogen, stress, trauma, dental procedures trigger attacks.
Hereditary Angioedema.typical_presentation: Recurrent non-pitting, non-pruritic, non-erythematous angioedema of skin, bowel (mimics acute abdomen), and larynx. NO urticaria. NO response to epinephrine or antihistamines.
Hereditary Angioedema.moa: C1-inhibitor deficiency → uncontrolled bradykinin production → increased vascular permeability. Bradykinin-mediated (NOT histamine-mediated) — explains lack of response to allergy treatments.
Hereditary Angioedema.step1_buzzwords: C4 low BETWEEN attacks (best screening test), no urticaria, no pruritus, no epinephrine response, bradykinin-mediated, ACE inhibitor contraindicated.
Hereditary Angioedema.lab_buzzwords: C4 critically low (even between attacks). C1-INH functional assay low. C1q normal (distinguishes from acquired C1-INH deficiency). Tryptase normal (not mast-cell).
Hereditary Angioedema.treatment: Acute: C1-INH concentrate, icatibant (bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist), or ecallantide. Prophylaxis: lanadelumab (anti-kallikrein), C1-INH infusions. NEVER give ACE inhibitors.

Polyarteritis Nodosa.who_gets_it: Adults 40-60, males > females. Associated with Hepatitis B (30%).
Polyarteritis Nodosa.risk_factors: Hepatitis B infection (most important), Hepatitis C, hairy cell leukemia.
Polyarteritis Nodosa.typical_presentation: Renal hypertension (infarcts not GN), mesenteric ischemia, testicular pain, mononeuritis multiplex, livedo reticularis, skin ulcers. ANCA negative.
Polyarteritis Nodosa.moa: Necrotizing vasculitis of medium-sized arteries — fibrinoid necrosis of the vessel wall WITHOUT immune complex deposition. Spares capillaries → NO glomerulonephritis.
Polyarteritis Nodosa.step1_buzzwords: Medium vessel vasculitis, NO glomerulonephritis, ANCA negative, microaneurysms on angiography ('string of beads'), HBV association.
Polyarteritis Nodosa.lab_buzzwords: ANCA negative (critical). Elevated ESR/CRP. Hematuria WITHOUT casts. HBsAg positive in 30%. Angiography: saccular microaneurysms at bifurcations.
Polyarteritis Nodosa.treatment: Steroids + cyclophosphamide. HBV-associated: antiviral therapy + plasmapheresis (NOT long-term immunosuppression). Avoid ACE inhibitors in renal artery involvement.

Susac Syndrome.who_gets_it: Young adults 20-50, females > males. Frequently misdiagnosed as MS, psychiatric illness, or viral encephalitis.
Susac Syndrome.risk_factors: Autoimmune endotheliopathy — anti-endothelial cell antibodies targeting microvasculature of brain, retina, cochlea.
Susac Syndrome.typical_presentation: Classic triad: encephalopathy (confusion, memory loss, psychiatric symptoms) + branch retinal artery occlusions (BRAOs in DIFFERENT eyes) + sensorineural hearing loss (low/mid frequencies).
Susac Syndrome.moa: Autoimmune occlusion of microvasculature of the brain, retina, and cochlea. Anti-alpha-SNAP antibodies identified in some cases.
Susac Syndrome.step1_buzzwords: Snowball lesions in CENTRAL corpus callosum (vs. peripheral in MS), Gass plaques on fundoscopy, BRAOs in multiple vessels, triad of encephalopathy + vision + hearing.
Susac Syndrome.lab_buzzwords: MRI: snowball lesions in corpus callosum body/genu. Fluorescein angiography: BRAOs with Gass plaques. Anti-AECA positive. All infectious and coag workups negative.
Susac Syndrome.treatment: Aggressive immunosuppression: high-dose steroids + mycophenolate or azathioprine + IVIG. Aspirin for retinal protection. Relapses common — long-term maintenance required.

Mesenteric Ischemia (Acute).who_gets_it: Elderly with A-fib, low cardiac output, or hypercoagulable states. Recent cardioversion is a trigger.
Mesenteric Ischemia (Acute).risk_factors: Atrial fibrillation (SMA embolism most common cause — 50%), low EF, hypercoagulable state, atherosclerosis (thrombosis), vasopressors (non-occlusive).
Mesenteric Ischemia (Acute).typical_presentation: Severe periumbilical pain OUT OF PROPORTION to physical exam (soft abdomen early). Bloody diarrhea is LATE. Atrial fibrillation on EKG. Elevated lactate.
Mesenteric Ischemia (Acute).moa: SMA embolism (50%) or thrombosis (25%) → bowel ischemia → mucosal infarction → bacterial translocation → sepsis. Every hour of delay = more bowel necrosis.
Mesenteric Ischemia (Acute).step1_buzzwords: Pain out of proportion to exam, A-fib, elevated lactate, pneumatosis intestinalis (late/ominous), elevated phosphate (cell death).
Mesenteric Ischemia (Acute).lab_buzzwords: Lactate >4 (severe ischemia), leukocytosis, elevated phosphate, CT angiography: SMA filling defect. Normal lipase (rules out pancreatitis).
Mesenteric Ischemia (Acute).treatment: Emergency CT angiography → catheter-directed thrombolysis or surgical embolectomy. Bowel resection for necrotic segments. Broad-spectrum antibiotics. Mortality >50% with delays.

Adrenal Crisis.who_gets_it: Patients on chronic steroids who abruptly stop; Addison's patients under physiologic stress; bilateral adrenal hemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen).
Adrenal Crisis.risk_factors: Abrupt glucocorticoid withdrawal (most common), physiologic stress in known adrenal insufficiency, meningococcemia (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage).
Adrenal Crisis.typical_presentation: Refractory hypotension unresponsive to fluids, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, eosinophilia. Hyperpigmentation if primary (elevated ACTH).
Adrenal Crisis.moa: Absolute cortisol deficiency → loss of vascular tone, impaired gluconeogenesis, impaired stress response. Mineralocorticoid deficiency (primary only) → salt-wasting, hyperkalemia.
Adrenal Crisis.step1_buzzwords: Eosinophilia (unique to adrenal crisis), refractory hypotension, hyponatremia + hyperkalemia (primary AI), hyperpigmentation, precipitated by steroid withdrawal or illness.
Adrenal Crisis.lab_buzzwords: Random cortisol <18 mcg/dL. ACTH stimulation: cortisol fails to rise >18. ACTH high (primary) or undetectable (secondary/steroid withdrawal). Hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia.
Adrenal Crisis.treatment: IV hydrocortisone 100mg STAT — do NOT wait for labs. Volume resuscitation with D5NS. Identify and treat precipitant. Sick day rules: double/triple steroid dose during illness or surgery.

Whipple Disease.who_gets_it: Middle-aged white males, especially farmers or outdoor workers. Extremely rare.
Whipple Disease.risk_factors: Tropheryma whipplei exposure — exact transmission unknown. Possible immune defect in bacterial clearance.
Whipple Disease.typical_presentation: Classic tetrad: diarrhea (steatorrhea) + weight loss + migratory non-erosive arthritis (precedes GI symptoms by years) + fever. CNS: oculomasticatory myorhythmia (pathognomonic eye movement).
Whipple Disease.moa: T. whipplei survives inside macrophages, blocking phagolysosome fusion — PAS-positive macrophages pack the lamina propria, blocking lymphatic drainage and nutrient absorption.
Whipple Disease.step1_buzzwords: PAS-positive macrophages in small bowel lamina propria, oculomasticatory myorhythmia, seronegative arthritis preceding GI symptoms, Tropheryma whipplei PCR.
Whipple Disease.lab_buzzwords: Biopsy: PAS-positive diastase-resistant macrophages (pathognomonic). PCR: T. whipplei in duodenal biopsy or CSF. Severe hypoalbuminemia. Elevated fecal fat.
Whipple Disease.treatment: Ceftriaxone IV x2 weeks → TMP-SMX x1-2 years (prevents relapse). CNS involvement: 2 years minimum. Check CSF PCR even without neuro symptoms — CNS relapse is devastating.

Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy.who_gets_it: Postmenopausal women (90%); triggered by intense emotional or physical stress.
Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy.risk_factors: Emotional stressor (death of loved one, argument), physical stress (surgery, stroke, sepsis), catecholamine surge.
Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy.typical_presentation: STEMI-like presentation with normal coronary arteries. Wall motion abnormality extends BEYOND any single coronary territory. Apical ballooning with hypercontractile base on echo/ventriculography.
Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy.moa: Catecholamine surge causes apical myocardial stunning — apical myocytes have more beta-2 receptors than basal myocytes, making the apex preferentially dysfunctional.
Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy.step1_buzzwords: Apical ballooning, octopus trap shape, normal coronaries, troponin elevated but DISPROPORTIONATELY LOW for wall motion abnormality, QTc prolongation.
Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy.lab_buzzwords: Troponin elevated but low relative to WMA severity. EKG: QTc prolonged, ST changes. Echo: apical ballooning + hypercontractile base. Angiography: normal coronaries.
Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy.treatment: Supportive — beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors. Anticoagulate if LV thrombus. Avoid catecholamines (worsen). EF recovers in 4-8 weeks. Recurrence ~10%. Prognosis generally excellent.

Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH.risk_factors: Somatic PIG-A mutation in hematopoietic stem cell. Associated with aplastic anemia.
Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH.typical_presentation: Morning hemoglobinuria (dark urine clearing by afternoon), pancytopenia, and thrombosis in unusual sites (hepatic veins = Budd-Chiari, portal vein, cerebral veins). Most common cause of Budd-Chiari in young patients.
Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH.moa: PIG-A mutation → absent GPI-anchor proteins (CD55, CD59) on RBC surface → unregulated complement activation → intravascular hemolysis + platelet activation + thrombosis.
Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH.step1_buzzwords: Morning hemoglobinuria, GPI-anchor deficiency, CD55/CD59 absent on flow cytometry, Budd-Chiari in young patient, pancytopenia + hemolysis.
Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH.lab_buzzwords: Flow cytometry: absent CD55 and CD59 on RBCs and granulocytes (diagnostic). LDH markedly elevated, haptoglobin undetectable, hemoglobinuria on UA. Ham test (historical).
Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH.treatment: Eculizumab (anti-C5 complement inhibitor) — dramatically reduces hemolysis and thrombosis. Anticoagulation for thrombosis. Bone marrow transplant is curative. Supplement iron/folate.

Spinal Dural Arteriovenous Fistula.who_gets_it: Males over 50 — most common spinal vascular malformation.
Spinal Dural Arteriovenous Fistula.risk_factors: Idiopathic in most cases. Rarely post-traumatic or iatrogenic.
Spinal Dural Arteriovenous Fistula.typical_presentation: Slowly progressive myelopathy — ascending leg weakness, hyperreflexia, Babinski, urinary incontinence. Symptoms worsen with exercise or standing. Frequently misdiagnosed as spinal stenosis or MS.
Spinal Dural Arteriovenous Fistula.moa: Abnormal AV connection at the dural sleeve of a nerve root → venous hypertension → progressive spinal cord ischemia (Foix-Alajouanine syndrome if untreated).
Spinal Dural Arteriovenous Fistula.step1_buzzwords: Serpiginous flow voids on dorsal cord surface (T2 MRI), progressive UMN myelopathy, no cord compression on MRI, venous hypertension, Foix-Alajouanine.
Spinal Dural Arteriovenous Fistula.lab_buzzwords: MRI: serpentine flow voids dorsal to cord + T2 cord signal change. Spinal angiography (gold standard): identifies feeding artery and fistulous point. EMG: UMN pattern.
Spinal Dural Arteriovenous Fistula.treatment: Endovascular embolization or surgical ligation of the fistula. Early treatment critical — delays cause irreversible cord damage. Neurological recovery partial — the longer the delay, the worse the outcome.

Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection (.who_gets_it: Young women (mean age 44) with NO traditional cardiac risk factors. Peripartum period is highest risk.
Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection (.risk_factors: Peripartum state, fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD), connective tissue disorders, intense exercise, emotional stress. NOT atherosclerosis.
Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection (.typical_presentation: STEMI or ACS in a young woman with NO cardiac risk factors. Peripartum or perimenopausal. Normal or near-normal coronary anatomy on initial angiography.
Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection (.moa: Spontaneous intramural hematoma or intimal tear creates a false lumen that compresses the true coronary lumen → ischemia. Type 2 (intramural hematoma without tear) is most common — missed on angiography alone.
Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection (.step1_buzzwords: Young woman, no risk factors, peripartum, FMD, normal coronaries on angio but IVUS/OCT shows intramural hematoma, smooth-walled narrowing without plaque.
Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection (.lab_buzzwords: Troponin markedly elevated (true MI). OCT/IVUS: intramural hematoma with false lumen. Angiography alone underestimates — intravascular imaging required. FMD on renal/iliac angiography.
Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection (.treatment: Conservative management — NO PCI (propagates dissection), NO thrombolytics. Beta-blockers to reduce aortic wall stress. Aspirin. Cardiac rehab. Screen for FMD. Recurrence rate ~20% — long-term cardiology follow-up.

IgG4-Related Disease.who_gets_it: Middle-aged to elderly males (60s). Multiorgan involvement — frequently misdiagnosed as cancer or Sjogren's.
IgG4-Related Disease.risk_factors: Unknown trigger. Dramatic steroid response distinguishes from malignancy.
IgG4-Related Disease.typical_presentation: Tumefactive lesions in multiple organs: pancreas (autoimmune pancreatitis), salivary/lacrimal glands (Mikulicz), orbits (pseudotumor), kidneys (TIN), retroperitoneum (fibrosis), bile ducts, aorta. ANA/SSA/SSB NEGATIVE.
IgG4-Related Disease.moa: IgG4-secreting plasma cells infiltrate tissues → storiform fibrosis + obliterative phlebitis + tissue destruction. TH2 and T-regulatory cell dysregulation.
IgG4-Related Disease.step1_buzzwords: Storiform fibrosis + obliterative phlebitis + >10 IgG4+ plasma cells/HPF (pathognomonic biopsy triad). Serum IgG4 >135 mg/dL. Dramatic steroid response. ANA/SSA/SSB negative.
IgG4-Related Disease.lab_buzzwords: Serum IgG4 >135 mg/dL (sensitive but not specific). Biopsy: storiform fibrosis + obliterative phlebitis + IgG4+ plasma cells. Eosinophilia common. Normal lipase despite pancreatic mass.
IgG4-Related Disease.treatment: High-dose prednisone — nearly universal dramatic response (diagnostic and therapeutic). Rituximab for refractory/relapsing disease. Steroid-sparing: mycophenolate or azathioprine. Always biopsy to exclude malignancy first.

Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS).who_gets_it: Patients started on antipsychotics (typical > atypical) or after rapid dose escalation. Also seen with abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic drugs (levodopa).
Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS).risk_factors: High-potency typical antipsychotics (haloperidol, fluphenazine), rapid dose escalation, dehydration, agitation, heat, prior NMS episode.
Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS).typical_presentation: The FALTER tetrad: Fever (>38.5C) + Altered mental status + Lead-pipe rigidity (severe) + autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis). Plus massively elevated CK from rhabdomyolysis.
Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS).moa: Dopamine D2 receptor blockade in the striatum (rigidity), hypothalamus (hyperthermia, autonomic instability), and limbic system (AMS). Results in uncontrolled muscle contraction and heat generation.
Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS).step1_buzzwords: Lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, antipsychotic exposure, CK >1000, low serum iron (characteristic), myoglobinuria. Distinguished from serotonin syndrome by rigidity > tremor and slower onset.
Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS).lab_buzzwords: CK markedly elevated (often >10,000). Myoglobinuria (brown urine, dipstick blood with no RBCs). Low serum iron. Leukocytosis. AKI from rhabdomyolysis. QTc prolongation.
Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS).treatment: STOP the offending agent immediately. ICU. Aggressive cooling. IV dantrolene (muscle relaxant — blocks SR calcium release). Bromocriptine (dopamine agonist). Benzos for agitation. Do NOT restart antipsychotics for 2 weeks minimum.
